questions.question:
Throughout the excerpt, Sal watches the Winterbottom family interact and then reflects on what matters, ‘in the course of a lifetime.’

According to the last paragraph, how does watching the Winterbottom family help Sal understand the difference between things that matter and things that don't matter in life? Use evidence from the text to support your response.

responses.content: Well, she is realizing that what she thought didn't matter if it matters and it's like the paragraph if your mom left you would feel bad now she can understand what matters and what doesn't matter and for sure she feels bad because she won't be able to be with her mom but now she realizes that things are wrong

Well, she is realizing that what she thought didn't matter if it matters and it's like the paragraph if your mom left you would feel bad now she can understand what matters and what doesn't matter and for sure she feels bad because she won't be able to be with her mom but now she realizes that things are wrong She is realizing what she did to her mom and that apparently didn't matter to her but now she knows what was wrong Your father was over there yesterday," Phoebe said. "I saw him. He'd better watch out. What would you do if Mrs. Cadaver chopped up your father? Would you go live with your mother?"

Well, she is realizing that what she thought didn't matter if it matters and it's like the paragraph if your mom left you would feel bad now she can understand what matters and what doesn't matter and for sure she feels bad because she won't be able to be with her mom but now she realizes that things are wrong  It surprised me when she said that, reminding me that I had told Phoebe nothing about my mother. "Yes, I suppose I would go live with her." That was impossible and I knew it, but for some reason I could not tell Phoebe that, so I lied. What I started doing was remembering the day before my mother left. I did not know it was to be her last day home. Several times that day, my mother asked me if I wanted to walk up in the fields with her. It was drizzling outside, and I was cleaning out my desk, and I just did not feel like going. "Maybe later," I kept saying. When she asked me for about the tenth time, I said, "No! I don't want to go. Why do you keep asking me?" I don't know why I did that. I didn't mean anything by it, but that was one of the last memories she had of me, and I wished I could take it back.
